item.title: Role of Youth in Addressing the Climate Crisis in Nepal - Webinar
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: In June 2020 Digo Bikas Institute brought together young Nepali climate activists working at local, regional and international level to discuss the role of Nepali youth in the climate movement. The webinar touched on policy, politics, organizing, and challenges that young activists have to face while trying to further the climate agenda in Nepal.  The webinar started with a discussion on challenges of working as youth activists. Sagarika Bhatta founder of Powershift Nepal highlighted the fact that although youth are energetic, they are not consistent and have a difficult time earning trust and credibility. Similarly, climate activist Bindu Bhandari emphasized the need for youth-led movements to be well informed about the issues they are advocating. In the case of Nepal, it means keeping up with Nepal’s commitment outlined in its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) and relevant laws, and connecting with regional networks of activists.  The conversation showed that the work of Nepali climate activists has created prioritization and streamlining of climate issues in Nepal. Shreya K.C from Nepalese Youth for Climate Action explained how NYCA was focused on mobilizing the youth to put pressure on policy-makers to take action. NYCA has organized letter writing and calling campaigns, lobbied to influence the content of Nepal’s NDC and declared plans at their national conference to push for assessment of climate change adaptation policies in local governments.  Prayash Adhikari and Tanuja Pandey pointed out that climate activists are often considered extremists because of their participation in protests. The solution that they proposed was to collaborate with schools, and other participants to make sure that protests are also a learning opportunity and people are familiarized more with the urgency of the issue. All of the participants highlighted the lack of political will to implement climate-friendly policies as a major hindrance for the youth and climate movement in Nepal. For example, Nepal’s government’s commitment to promote electric vehicles in the NDC while simultaneously increasing the tax on EV’s (now rolled-back), and the decision to go ahead with Nijgadh International Airport’s construction despite a stay order from the Supreme Court and civil society outcry. Anish Shrestha who has been involved in regional and international climate movements said that exchange of knowledge between these networks on how to translate public interest into political movement is crucial to inspire change. He also mentioned Nepali youth have a lot to contribute to regional caucuses so that issues of the region are represented in international climate conferences.  Nepal has a youth population of 44% therefore an inter-generational issue such as the climate emergency should be at the forefront of every policy decision. Nepal is lucky to have activists who are contributing to the movement.  You can listen to the full version of the webinar on our Facebook Page.

item.title: Sharing programme on sustainable urban mobility in Province 1
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: On the occasion of the World Bicycle Forum 2020, a virtual side event on the Sharing Program among the Local Government of Province 1 on Sustainable Urban Mobility was organized by Digo Bikas Institute with Pack and Pedal, a bicycle shop. Virtual sharing program which took place on Monday 21st September, 2020 was attended by Mr. Bhim Parajuli, the Mayor of Biratnagar Metropolitan City, Dwarik Lal Chaudhary, Mayor, Itahari, Sub-Metropolitan City, Ms. Bhoj Kumari Nepal, Deputy Mayor of Shiva Satakshi Rural Municipality, Som Nath Neupane, Chairperson of the Jhapa District Coordination Committee and Khadga Pariyar, Chairperson of the Udaypur District Coordination Committee. The meeting  resulted in scope for further collaboration at provincial scale in Province 1, while present mayors expressed commitment and call for assistance in building policy and programs of inclusive and sustainable urban intervention from civil society. The session also helped mobilize local youth groups and community members to work further in collaboration.

item.title: Nijgadh International Airport. People, Biodiversity and Future. What is at Stake?
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Digo Bikas Institute, Harin Nepal and Power Shift Nepal jointly organized a virtual roundtable discussion on the topic Nijgadh International Airport- People, Biodiversity and Future. What is atstake? The international airport at Nijgadh is one of the most contentious development projects in Nepal because  the  it threatens irreversible loss to biodiversity and the community. Sanjay Adhikari, a public interest litigator who has been engaged in the Nijgadh international airport public litigation, and one of the petitioners to the supreme court, mentioned that the environment impact assessment report (EIA) of the airport is flawed and should be called environment impact non-assessment. The destruction of the environment and biodiversity and displacement of the community in the proposed airport site questions the fundamental right to live in a healthy environment as enshrined in the constitution of Nepal. He also mentioned that the Supreme Court has given an order not to chop the trees for the construction whereas there has been a conflicting decision from the parliamentary committee and President’s office to start the project. Adhikari urged participants to stand together and raise the voice about the ecocide being committed in the name of the development through Nijgadh international airport.

item.title: Car Free Cities Alliance Strategy Meeting - Bandipur
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Digo Bikas Institute team joined a two-day car-free cities alliance strategy meeting in Bandipur. The aim of the meeting was to review the work of the car-free cities alliance globally and of the Nepal chapter. The strategy meeting was attended by the representatives from member organizations based in India, Bangladesh and the Netherlands. The meeting helped define working modality for the CCA core team and identify approaches to coordinate with countries and cities that are involved in Car Free development on different scales globally. The meeting was conducted in Bandipur, one of the more popular Car Free destinations in Nepal, where a meeting was also arranged between the mayor of the municipality and the CCA team. [caption id="attachment_725" align="alignnone" width="960"] At CCA strategy meeting[/caption] The strategy meeting worked to define objectives, vision, the base for toolkit/platforms, CCA brand and website, Carfree-climate campaign strategy, projects and programs, funding, core team management functions and action plans for the coming year.

item.title: Challenges in Conversion and Development of Vehicle Free Zones in Nepal
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: A round table discussion was conducted in Kathmandu between government officials from the Urban Ministry and municipalities, academics, the private sector, activists, and journalists who have been engaged on issues of urban development, walkability, bicycle and non-motorized transport promotion, and the members of Car-free Cities Alliance. This round table discussion explored the present status of vehicle-free efforts in Nepal and analyzed the present and future challenges in the conversion and development of Vehicle-Free zones in Nepal. The discussion also gave a way for collaboration with the Urban Ministry and New Town Development Project office to explore further cooperation possibilities for Car-free development in new towns and smart city projects led by the Urban Ministry.

item.title: Dhapsung Water Supply: Phase 1
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: To address the water shortage and provide water for irrigation purposes, the Dhapsung water supply was initiated as per the needs of the Dhapsung community. The Dhapsung water user community was formed by the people of the Dhapsung community, and the community itself oversaw the construction, quality of the material, and conducted the monitoring and evaluation of the water supply project. The water supply will benefit 53 households of Dhapsung village. [caption id="attachment_733" align="alignnone" width="1600"] Construction of Dhapsung water-supply[/caption] The project construction was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and could not be completed on time. The DBI team will be working to provide a water tap to individual households in 2021 once all the construction work related to water supply and water supply pipe fitting is completed.

item.title: Policy Engagement with government on Vehicle Free Developments
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: A design thinking workshop was organized for the community members of Kilagal, a core Kathmandu neighborhood in partnership with the City Planning Commission. The workshop helped to explore different strategic intervention plans to implement gradual vehicle regulation in the community. The workshop also helped to bridge the connection between community members and the city planning commission personnels. Participation was done online as well as in-person. After consultation with the Parliamentarians of Bagmati Province, a session with ward officials of different wards in the historic core of Kathmandu Metropolitan City is being organized. The response of the meetings will then help to build the strategy and content of the workshop. A municipal level workshop has been identified as a necessary step before organizing a national level workshop as stated in the Livable Cities Nepal annual plan.    The Digo Bikas team also had a series of meetings with Biratnagar Metropolitan City on vehicle-free development and non-motorized transport. DBI was successful in convincing Biratnagar Metropolitan City to have a bicycle master plan in the 2020/21-year policy and plan red book. DBI with its partner organization will be assisting the BMC to conduct a survey and prepare an assessment report on possible areas for bicycle lanes in Biratnagar. Further DBI will provide technical assistance in identifying possible areas for vehicle-free zones and vehicle-free night markets in Biratnagar.

item.title: Webinar on Oil Drilling in Nepal
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Digo Bikas Institute and Power Shift Nepal organized two virtual discussions on the topic related to oil drilling, as Nepal is in the process of exploring oil and starting oil drilling with technical assistance from the Government of China. First virtual discussion was on the topic “Impacts of Fracking” where scientist and activist Esteban G. Servat shared his global experience of the fracking technology for oil extraction, how communities can be displaced and how the water and land will be polluted in the name of the oil extraction. He also encouraged youth and activists to advocate to prevent the possible serious impact on the people, land and water. The second virtual sharing session was focused on “Mining and Oil extraction impact” where Dr. Esther Figuero, filmmaker, environment and social injustice activist shared her experience from Jamaica. She shared how  the community was impacted by the mining project and further shared the story of resistance from the community against the mining company and the government.

item.title: Research on Sustainable Biratnagar Electric City Rickshaw Stand and Shade with Open Public Space
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Digo Bikas Institute is researching Sustainable Biratnagar City Rickshaw Stand and Shade with Open Public Space. Under this ongoing research, public spaces and parks in Biratnagar are being mapped to identify the best suitable position to construct the solar-powered city rickshaw stand near public space. [caption id="attachment_755" align="alignnone" width="4032"] Electric rickshaw, a sustainable and feasible means to urban mobility[/caption] Furthermore, key informants, focus group discussions and in-person interviews with the rickshaw riders, and the association of the rickshaw riders have been conducted to understand battery usage, current charging practice and monthly bills paid by the electric city rickshaw.

item.title: Seed distribution and collective greenhouse tunnel support to Dhapsung Mother’s Group
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Digo Bikas Institute supported Dhapsung Women’s group members with different varieties of vegetable seeds from local agriculture cooperatives and helped to build a collective greenhouse tunnel for the cultivation of the vegetables. [caption id="attachment_742" align="alignnone" width="960"] Collective tunnels[/caption] The vegetables from the Dhapsung village were sold to Kathmandu organics, a Kathmandu based company that sells organic products nationwide. For the sale of the vegetables, Dhapsung women’s group received 25,000 NPR. Dhapsung Women’s group is excited and motivated to engage in vegetable farming after their product has received a buyer.

item.title: DBI Relief Support - Mahadevtar, Kavre Palanchowk
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: In collaboration with Nari Chetna Kendra, Digo Bikas Institute provided relief support to the Mahadevtar Community, one of the communities highly impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic. The team of Nari Chetna Kendra, Roshi Rural Municipality Ward no 4 and the DBI team visited the community and provided 33 households 1 sack of rice, 5 kgs pulses, 2 litres of oil, and  1 packet of salt. The community was identified based on the conversation with Nari Chetna Kendra, as NCK has been working with the women-led cooperative in Mahadevtar.

item.title: DBI Relief Support- Dhapsung, Sindhupalchowk
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Digo Bikas Institute provided relief support to the community of the Dhapsung, Total of 52 households of Dhapsung were supported with a 1 sack of rice of 25 kg, 500 gm of nutrela, 2 kgs pulses, 2 litres of oil and 2 packets of salt each. [caption id="attachment_772" align="alignnone" width="750"] Covid relief support in Dhapsung, Helambu.[/caption]

item.title: Virtual 9th WORLD BICYCLE FORUM, 2020
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: DBI was one of the 55 partner organizations (international, local, government agency, youth groups, academic institutions and NGOs) of World Bicycle Forum 2020, a virtual event led by the national bicycle campaigners’ network. The event was mobilized as a very important platform to take the urban agenda to the government agencies at the national, provincial and local levels. It was also very productive to connect and mobilize primary stakeholders and bicycle groups to engage in broader urban issues. The event had 450 participants such as volunteers, panelists, organizers and guests, while its online engagement by the end of the event was 70,000, as all events including multiple pre-events were shared live on social media globally. Of the 56 events organized in the forum, many sessions were organized by DBI in collaboration with other stakeholders. Similarly, DBI was directly involved with the execution of a session through which 12 city mayors from different parts of Nepal participated in a discussion about the bicycle agenda, led by local bicycle groups, where the scope of intervention and local strategy for implementation were discussed. There will be a need to further strengthen; local community groups to self-organize for effective lobbying capacity. As part of the forum, collaboration was also done with the municipal association of Nepal, to build a national scale municipal collaboration for bicycle intervention by the local government. A year plan for 7 provincial consultation programs for municipalities of the 7 provinces has been planned out through the sessions and post-event discussion. As part of the World bicycle Forum, many sessions with the involvement of the DBI team were facilitated to build the capacity of the bicycle community at the national and regional levels. Some of the other sessions included sharing from Continental Bicycle network from Europe and Africa, National Bicycle network from Brazil, and Peru, Sessions by Carfree Cities Alliance, BYCS India network sharing.

item.title: Global Solidarity - Fight Inequality
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Online actions were organized in global solidarity to fight inequality and called on business, government and civil society leaders meeting at the World Economic Forum (WEF) to urgently address climate change and inequality. Abhishek Shrestha, Program Director, DBI, stressed that the same economic system that has given rise to and thrives on inequality has also created the problem of climate change and mentioned that the fight for climate justice is a fight against inequality. He further added the dominant global economic system involves profit-driven extraction, production, and trade that is heavily dependent on fossil fuel energy and the main reason for the global greenhouse emission and climate crisis. He called on everyone to Act Now on Climate Justice and stressed that tackling the climate crisis and inequality must go hand in hand. Sarah Ranabhat, Nepal Climate Convergence alumni stated that the most responsible for climate change i.e. corporations and the wealthy, industrialized countries should do their fair share to solve the climate crisis and meet their obligations to enable people and communities to deal with its impacts. Participants of the online action chanted 1. Fight inequality and 2. Act for climate justice now.

item.title: Environment Activist demand justice for first Envrionment martyr Dilip on Martyr's day
item.dc:creator: digoadmin

item.title: Activist handed letter to NHRC demanding independent investigation of murder of Dilip Mahato
item.dc:creator: digoadmin

item.title: Weekly Reading List - February 21, 2021
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Money Is the Oxygen on Which the Fire of Global Warming Burns by Bill McKibben Bill McKibben discusses how the global financial industry is a key driver in sustaining the fossil fuel economy. Climate change to cause frequent landslides in the Himalayan region, including Nepal, says NASA by Chandan Kumar Mandal in The Kathmandu Post An added consequence of climate change in mountain countries. दैलेखमा पेट्रोलियम अन्वेषण : ड्रिल गर्ने कार्य तिहारपछि मात्र - गोरखापत्र नेपाल Nepal government’s reckless and controversial plan to drill for oil gets postponed. Who is Disha Ravi, the climate activist arrested by Delhi police? by Vijayata Lalwani for Scroll.in Bengaluru-based climate-activist was arrested in India this week for speaking up for Indian farmers. Women are seen as victims in the fight for climate justice, and that needs to change by Prasiddhi Shrestha for the Record “By focusing our attention on women as victims, we have effectively taken the conversation away from the potential that these very women have as agents of change.”

item.title: Nepali CSO's made submission on the Climate Leaders Summit
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
21st April 2021 To Mr. John F. Kerry United States Special Presidential Envoy for Climate Washington D.C., United States CC Randy W. Berry, Ambassador, U.S. Embassy in Nepal Maharajgunj, Kathmandu, Nepal   Dear Mr. Kerry We would like to start by thanking President Biden’s commitment to the global climate movement and by congratulating the United States for rejoining the Paris Agreement. We also appreciate President Biden’s effort to organize the Leaders Summit on Climate, hence looking forward to a stronger concerted climate action. These are certainly first-hand examples of the Biden administration’s deep concern on the global climate crisis. As much as we appreciate those efforts, truth be told, those were long overdue. The United States should have taken this leadership role long ago, but unfortunately, international cooperation on climate was deprioritized by the US for several years. The United States is currently one of the top emitters in the world, accounting for 15% of the global CO2 emission (IEA, 2018). It also holds the record of being the all time largest and worst GHG emitter on the planet if cumulative CO2 emission since 1750 is taken into account (CarbonBrief, 2019). Because of this, the US bears a greater imperative for curbing its CO2 output and a greater moral responsibility for the impacts of global warming, for which unfortunately, countries like Nepal are now suffering. We believe it is time for the US to turn their backs on their polluting pasts and become true climate leaders by immediately ending all support for fossil fuel projects overseas. We welcome President Biden’s commitment to galvanize efforts by the major economies towards emissions reductions and keep the 1.5 global target degree within reach. Nepal is facing huge economic suffering from the Climate crisis and extreme weather events. Latest reports reveal that estimated costs of the impacts is equivalent to 1.5–2% GDP/year, approximately US$270–360 million/year in 2013 prices and is much higher in extreme years, rising to 5% or more. Each passing year we have increased numbers and intense flood, drought, landslides, forest fire, decrease in agricultural productivity, displacement of the communities, among them are women, indigenous people, youth and people with disability, who are most impacted by the climate crisis. Undoubtedly, the suffering of the climate impacts is higher and irreversible every year in Nepal. Nepal has been leading adaptation and resilience efforts and moving towards a low carbon development pathway and also called for global gathering “Sagarmatha sambad” for international cooperation among different states to tackle the crisis of our generation. We hope the Biden Administration will also make an effort to hear from countries that are not in the Climate Leaders Summit but are most vulnerable to the climate crisis. We look forward to see beginning of real leadership that the world needs from the US, specifically through the following demands:  
  1. The United States must push up Global Climate Finance, which is falling far short of $100 billion pledges at Paris: The Annex-1 countries including the United States never met their pledge of providing $100 billion annually for adaptation and mitigation through the Green Climate Fund (GCF). This amount is not a grant or donation, it is the part of the reparations for the huge ecological debt developed countries owe to the developing nations. We demand our legitimate reparations from the United States and urge the Biden Administration will fulfill their commitment.
  2. We demand the US government to fulfill their fair shares of climate action based on equity and science. The US should set ambitious NDC targets and aim to achieve real zero by 2050, which can be done through full domestic decarbonization by 2030, and urgent delivery of climate finance for people and communities in the Global South, like Nepal.
  3. US financial institutions must stop funding fossil fuels and lead the fossil fuel divestment movement. Four US banks - JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo, Citi Bank, and Bank of America - are the world’s four biggest lenders and underwriters to the fossil fuel industry since the Paris Agreement. These institutions are essentially undermining President Biden’s pledge to the Paris Agreement and leadership towards a decarbonized society. If we are to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees, the US must end fossil fuel finance and lead efforts to divest from coal, oil and gas. Over a thousand institutions globally worth almost 8 trillion USD have committed to divest from the world’s biggest oil, coal and gas companies (org, 2021). Unfortunately, US investors and technology suppliers fall short on the global commitment list and are still continuing its business helping to expand fossil-fuel based power projects globally We urge the Biden administration to set policies geared towards the immediate withdrawal of all fossil fuel investments.
  4. US should take a leadership role for a global transition to a new clean energy economy: The US is home to one of the largest and fastest-growing renewable energy markets, with advanced technology innovations, creating job opportunities and boosting economic growth. We urge the US financiers and tech giants to get involved with renewable opportunities in Nepal to serve the mutual interests of both countries to build a decarbonized society.
It is indeed time to act and we the climate justice groups in the Global South, together with the rest of the world are watching and waiting for the US to lead the urgently needed Climate Action once and for all. . Signatory:
  1. Digo Bikas Institute
  2. Environment Law Society, Nepal
  3. Indigenous Women's Legal Awareness Group
  4. Jagaran Nepal
  5. Karnali Integrated Rural Development and Research Centre
  6. National Indigenous Disabled Women Association Nepal
  7. Power Shift Nepal
  8. Youth For Environment Education And Development Foundation

item.title: Sound The Alarm on the Climate Emergency and Demand Climate Justice
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Digo Bikas Institute joined the coordinated day of actions organized by the Asia Pacific Movement on Debt and Development (APMDD) with a big call for climate justice. The theme for coordinated action on January 15 was “Sound the Alarm - Climate Emergency! Act now for Climate Justice." Observing the health protocols of COVID-19, a virtual rally was organized. The following slogans were chanted during the online “Sound the Alarm” climate rally.
  1. End the age of fossil fuels! Democratic, renewable and clean energy systems for people and communities. 
  2. People and planet before profit! Defend our rights to food, land, water, safety, services and a healthy planet.
  3. Ecological restoration not nature-bashing solutions. 
  4. Equity and fair sharing of climate action! Climate finance as reparations and payment of climate debt.
  5. Real zero not net-zero! No to false solutions. 
  6. No climate justice without gender justice.

item.title: Tribute to Nepal's First Environmental Martyr Dilip Mahato was organized
item.dc:creator: digoadmin

item.title: COP 30 Side Event
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
.       
UNFCCC COP 30 Official Side event, Belem, Para, Brazil
13th November 2025
Digo Bikas Institute, together with NIDWAN and SAMATA, organized the UNFCCC COP 30 official side event on the topic “ANOTHER ROUND OF NDCS: BUT WHERE’S THE MONEY?”. The event included sharing from Nepal, including panelists Claire Miranda, APMDD; Thilak Kariyawasam, FIAN, Sri Lanka; Pratima Gurung, NIDWAN, Nepal; Pavel Partha, BARCIK, Bangladesh; R. Ravi of Samata, India & was moderated by Prayash Adhikari, Digo Bikas Institute, Nepal.
Speakers share their engagements on the NDC process, lived realities from the grassroots community, highlighting the need for Adequate, New, Additional, non-debt-creating climate finance, without which the crisis will exacerbate and the discussion will be limited to papers and commitments.

item.title: COP 30 Booth
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Official Booth at COP 30 Digo Bikas Institute showcased its research and publication, along with photos and videos of various activities that DBI has been doing in recent years.

item.title: Asia Pacific Social Forum 2025
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: From 1st - 5th November 2025, Digo Bikas Institute participated in the Asia Pacific Social Forum. The engagements included leading the Peace Rally and various parallel events throughout the forum.

item.title: The first Carfree Day in Kopche, Dhankuta
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
The first Carfree Day in Kopche, Dhankuta on 8th November 2025 saw the street become a dynamic public place for connection and reflection, which was brought to life with traditional games, cultural showcases, and authentic food stalls, culminating in a communal Newari Bhoj right on the chowk. A Sensory Walk encouraged a deeper engagement with the environment, while the Sukul Sambaad provided a crucial space to decompress and collectively plan a way forward. A significant first step was also taken towards a sister relationship proposal between the Kopche Punarjaagaran Abhiyaan and Jiwanta Hadigaun Abhiyaan.
Lasting impact springs from community ownership. Having been with both the Kopche and Hadigaun campaigns from the start, Digo Bikas Institute is proud to have facilitated this collaboration.
This event was a collaboration between Dhankuta Municipality, Digo Bikas Institute, ICIMOD, HUSADEC, Nepal Tourism Board, Community Homestay Network, and Smart Paani, driven by the incredible effort of the Kopche Punarjaagaran Abhiyaan.

item.title: Planet Local Summit 2025
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: DBI participated in the Planet Local Summit in Ladakh to learn, share, and connect on the essence of Localization. From sessions on Asian Localization Stories, hosting our booth, to immersive village visits emphasizing the depth of community-led, culturally sensitive practices, and engaging with Himalayan Institute of Alternatives, Ladakh (HIAL) fellows - this participation deepened our commitment to building livable, localized, and resilient futures.

item.title: Draw The Line Action (Kathmandu and Biratnagar)
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
People's Climate Mobilization came together to Draw The Line
Floods, droughts, storms, and heatwaves are hitting our communities harder every year. Prices for food and energy keep rising, while a few continue to profit at the expense of our lands, rivers, and air. Indigenous communities, farmers, and youth are speaking up: our future is at stake. We'll stand united for that fair, resilient future with clean energy on hand.
In solidarity with actions across the globe, we here in Nepal stand together with a common message that this world belongs to all of us.

item.title: Civil Society and movements submitted a 3-tier amendment suggestion on "जलवायु वित्त परिचालन कार्यविधि २०८२"
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Civil Society and movements submitted a 3-tier amendment suggestion on "जलवायु वित्त परिचालन कार्यविधि २०८२"
Civil Society Organizations, movements, networks, and marginalized groups had a round to discussion and submitted 3 tier amendment suggestion on “जलवायु वित्त परिचालन नीति २०८२”

item.title: Participatory Planning Process for Climate resilient Future in Belauri, Far-western Nepal
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
We’re excited to share that a two-day Climate Change Vulnerability and Risk Assessment (VRA) from right to food perspective was successfully completed in Belauri Municipality, jointly organized by Digo Bikas Institute (DBI) and Food First International Network–Nepal (FIAN).
With the active participation of municipal officials, local representatives, and community members, the assessment helped identify key climate hazards, and issues vulnerable groups, and livelihoods most at risk.
Together, we strengthened local understanding of how climate change impacts our communities — and highlighted the importance of integrating right to food and food sovereignty into participatory planning for a climate resilient future leading to the recommendation of community owned adaptation options

item.title: People’s Workshop on NDC
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Digo Bikas Institute (DBI) and National Indigenous Disabled Women Association Nepal (NIDWAN) collectively organised “People’s Workshop on NDC” to discuss the NDC process in Nepal from a Climate justice lens, connecting NDC with grassroots and marginalised communities. Participants shared the people’s observations on the NDC and its process, and discussed how Civil Society Organisations (CSOs), grassroots movements, youth networks, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, and other stakeholders contribute to the process further.
Dr. Maheshwor Dhakal, Chief, Climate Change Management Division, MoFE, presented Climate Change Policy, Naps and Nepal's NDC 3.0, which was followed by a group discussion on how NDC relates to our lives and livelihoods, its effective engagement and our contribution and expectation and way forward on the NDC process.

item.title: Debunking the Myths on Climate Justice
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
We successfully organized an engaging panel session, “Debunking the Myths on Climate Justice” during the 5th National Youth Mega Meet 2025 at Pragya Pratisthan, Kathmandu, on the occasion of International Youth Day.
The panel brought the five diverse voices: Pratima Gurung from the National Indigenous Disabled Women Association Nepal (NIDWAN), Abhishek Shrestha from the Fight Inequality Alliance (FIA), Laxmi Bhandari from the National Farmers Group Federation (NFGF), Naren Khatiwada from Youth Advocacy Nepal (YAN), and Omkar from Digo Bikas Institute (DBI) - with the moderation from Susmita Baidawar from Digo Bikas Institute (DBI).
From the perspectives of women, Youth, Indigenous leaders, people with disabilities, farmers, grassroots and global movements, activists, we challenged the false solutions and the false narrative of Climate Justice, promoting people-led, community-owned, justice-oriented climate solutions.
A huge thanks to all panelists, participants and collaborators for joining us in the collective effort to fight for Climate Justice.

item.title: GREEN CITY VOLUNTEER BIRATNAGAR MEET AND GREET PROGRAM 2025
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: The "Green City Volunteer Biratnagar Meet and Greet Program 2025" successfully united the 1st cohort with the newly formed 2nd Cohort Green City Volunteer Biratnagar in collaboration with the Biratnagar Metropolitan Office. The pioneers from the first cohort exchanged vital ground level insights through thematic presentations and one-on-one dialogues; the foundation for continued environmental action in Biratnagar was strengthened.

item.title: MONITORING MONDAY
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: Monitoring Monday, led by Green City Volunteers, focused on tracking the progress of plants planted across various schools and public spaces in collaboration with the Biratnagar Metropolitan Office, this initiative ensured that past plantation efforts continue to thrive, contributing to a Green, Clean, and Livable Biratnagar for All.

item.title: ORIENTATION ON GREEN AND LIVABLE PUBLIC SPACE TO GREEN CITY VOLUNTEER BIRATNAGAR 2ND COHORT
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: The “Orientation on Green and Livable Public Space” was successfully conducted on 7th August 2025 for the Green City Volunteer 2nd Cohort. The session focused on the vital role public spaces play in urban health and environment, equipping them with the knowledge of accessible and greener Biratnagar.

item.title: ORIENTATION ON SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT TO GREEN CITY VOLUNTEER BIRATNAGAR 2ND COHORT
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: The “Orientation on Solid Waste Management” was successfully conducted on 30th August 2025 with our Green City Volunteer 2nd Cohort. Mr. Punam Dahal, Chief Officer of the Waste Management & Cleanliness Branch, Biratnagar Metropolitan, shared key actions, principles, and ongoing efforts in Biratnagar’s waste management, highlighting the vital role of youth. Mrs. Madhu Rai also inspired volunteers with insights on community-led waste initiatives and how children, youth, and local groups can turn waste into reusable resources for a cleaner community.

item.title: FIELD VISIT TO KESHALIYA DUMPING SITE AND COMMUNITY, BIRATNAGAR
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: The Solid Waste Management Team from the Green City Volunteer Biratnagar 2nd Cohort conducted field visits to the Keshaliya Dumping Site and nearby communities to understand the ground realities and assess the current situation of residents affected by the dumping site in Biratnagar.

item.title: FIELD VISIT TO PUBLIC SPACES OF BIRATNAGAR
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded: The Green and Livable Public Space Team of the Green City Volunteer Biratnagar 2nd Cohort conducted a revisit to key public spaces, followed by a detailed mapping exercise to assess their current condition, challenges, and level of community engagement. The team aims to compile a comprehensive status report on public spaces across Biratnagar.

item.title: The Food Rights-Based VRA Tool Implementation in Sudurpaschim and Karnali Provinces
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Digo Bikas Institute in coordination with FIAN Nepal has implemented Vulnerabilty and Risk Assessment (VRA) tool in Kanchnapur (Belauri municipality); Surkhet (Chingad, Bheriganga and Simta rural municipalities) and Jajarkot (Shiwalaya rural municipality) of Sudurpaschim and Karnali provinces respectively. Implementation of the VRA tool was focused on collecting the data on how food rights and sovereignty has been challenged due to different climatic changes and disasters on concerned areas/communities.
This process involved especially three phases (i.e., pre-planning, implementation and future action). The pre-planning phase involved the formation of a knowledge group emphasizing community mobilization and organizing stakeholders before assessment begins. The implementation phase centered on vulnerability and capacity analysis, where communities collectively identify climate risks, food system impacts, and existing coping capacities. Similarly, the future action phase highlighted adaptation planning and advocacy, translating assessment findings into concrete adaptation measures and policy engagement. This bottom-up approach systematizes lived experiences that affected communities' food rights due to climate change into structured data on risk, adaptive capacity, and rights gaps, grounding climate analysis in local realities.

item.title: National Consultation on Just Transition Away from Fossil Fuels
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Digo Bikas Institute in collaboration with the Ministry of Forest and Environment(MoFE) and Clean Energy Nepal(CEN), organized the National Consultation on Just Transition Away from Fossil Fuels, Preparation for Santa Marta Conference on 9th April 2026.
The opening remarks were delivered by Dr. Maheshwar Dhakal, Chief of the Climate Change Management Division at MoFE, followed by a keynote presentation by Manjeet Dhakal. Mega Maskey, Asia Campaigner, delivered a brief presentation on the Fossil Fuel Treaty Initiative.
The panel discussion, moderated by Shreya K.C., brought together representatives from government, local authorities, and civil society. Panelists included Dr. Prakash Singh Thapa (MoFE), Ms. Parbata Bhatta (AEPC), Mr. Yugdeep Luitel (Itahari Municipality), and Mr. Abhishek Shrestha (Digo Bikas Institute). The discussion focused on Nepal’s energy transition challenges, policy gaps, and opportunities for multi-level collaboration.
An open floor session followed, facilitated by Omkar Subedi and Megha Maskey and the event concluded with the closing remarks from Mr. Raju Sapkota (MoFE).
#JustTransition
#santamartaconference
#climatejustice

item.title: Expression of Interest (EOI): Website Redesign, Development, and Maintenance Services
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Expression of Interest (EOI) for Website Redesign, Development, and Maintenance Services. Digo Bikas Institute invites qualified web development firms, creative agencies, or independent technical consultants to submit proposals for the professional architectural redesign, front-end optimization, and development of its official website (www.digobikas.org). The assignment involves executing a mobile-first UI/UX overhaul, constructing an advanced digital repository for research and policy materials, integrating distinct portals for programmatic pillars, and implementing eco-conscious, lightweight engineering practices. Background Digo Bikas Institute (DBI) is a prominent research and advocacy organization committed to promoting ecological sustainability and social equity at both policy and community levels in Nepal. Guided by the values of equity, fairness, justice, transparency, and inclusiveness, DBI undertakes rigorous research and action to advance alternative, local solutions to global environmental and socioeconomic challenges. Our core programmatic focus areas include:
  1. Rethinking Development: Promoting economic justice and practical activities that champion ecological sustainability.
  2. Climate Justice: Facilitating systemic transformation, policy analysis, and community-frontline networking.
  3. Sustainability Watch: Tracking the enforcement of environmental regulations and federal governance frameworks.
  4. Sustainability Education: Building a critical mass of informed citizens through targeted awareness, camps, and summer schools.
As our publication archives, community toolkits, and interactive campaigns grow, DBI requires a modern, secure, highly performant, and intuitive web presence that serves as a premier knowledge hub for policymakers, civil society organizations (CSOs), youth groups, and international climate justice partners. Scope of Work - The selected partner will be expected to deliver:
  • Comprehensive UI/UX Redesign: A mobile-first, highly accessible user experience optimized for varying bandwidth constraints across different provinces of Nepal.
  • Advanced Repository Infrastructure: A seamless, robust digital archive to host, categorize, and search specialized research papers, policy briefs, and vulnerability tools (e.g., studies on high-mountain GLOF events, fossil fuel transitions, and food rights-based toolkits).
  • Dynamic Program Integration: Structured, distinct portals for DBI's four programmatic pillars and dedicated spaces for public mobilizations like the People's Climate March and Climate Justice Summer School.
  • Eco-Conscious Web Engineering: Lightweight code frameworks and asset optimization to minimize page weights and the site's overall digital carbon footprint ("Green Web Design").
  • Content Migration & Training: Secure migration of all historical news, features, and logs from the current legacy database, followed by comprehensive CMS orientation for administrative staff.
 
  • How to Apply
Interested parties (companies or individuals) are requested to submit their application package containing:
    1. Registration Certificate
    2. VAT Registration Certificate
    3. Tax Clearance Certificate
    4. Company Profile (Work sample reference)
    5. Brief proposed methodology, work plan, and timeline
    6. Financial quotation (Lump sum, inclusive of all applicable costs)
    7. Contact details     
 
  • Timeline and Duration:
Will be discussed during the contract signing. Deadline for Submission: June 22, 2026.   Electronic submissions should be sent in PDF format via email with the subject line "EOI: DBI Website Design Services - [Your Entity Name]" to: ( hr@digobikas.org ) For technical or administrative inquiries regarding this call, contact: Digo Bikas Institute (DBI) Puja Prathisthan Margh, Mid Baneshwor, Kathmandu, Nepal Phone: +977-01-4610656 DBI reserves the right to accept or reject any or all expressions of interest without assigning any reason whatsoever. Only shortlisted candidates/firms will be contacted for the secondary Request for Proposal (RFP) process, which will include detailed financial bidding

item.title: Expression of Interest (EOI): Nepali Dubbing of “The Economics of Happiness”
item.dc:creator: digoadmin
item.content:encoded:
Expression of Interest (EOI) for Expression of Interest (EOI): Nepali Dubbing of “The Economics of Happiness”.

Digo Bikas Institute invites qualified media houses, dubbing studios, or individual professionals to submit proposals for the professional Nepali dubbing and language refinement of the award-winning 68-minute documentary, "The Economics of Happiness" (produced by Local Futures)

Scope of Work The selected firm/studio/company shall undertake the following tasks: 1) Review and Correction of Nepali Translation
  • Review the existing Nepali translated script/subtitle text.
  • Correct grammar, spelling, sentence flow, terminology, and contextual meaning.
  • Ensure the language is natural, clear, audience-friendly, and aligned with the tone of the documentary.
  • Maintain accuracy of original English meaning.
2) Voice Assessment and Casting Plan  
  • Carefully review the documentary.
  • Identify total number of voice artists required based on speakers, narration style, gender, tone, and character distinctions.
  • Recommend voice allocation plan (main narrator, male/female speakers, supporting voices, etc.).
  • Submit final voice artist list for approval before recording.
3) Voice Recording and Dubbing
  • Arrange voice artists.
  • Record clean studio-quality Nepali voice track
  • Synchronize dubbing with visuals and speaking pace.
  • Maintain emotional tone, and authenticity of original documentary.
  4) Editing, Mixing and Final Output
  • Mix dubbed audio with background music/effects.
  • Deliver broadcast-ready final master file.
 
  • How to Apply

Interested parties (companies or individuals) are requested to submit their application package containing:

    1. Registration Certificate
    2. VAT Registration Certificate
    3. Tax Clearance Certificate
    4. Company Profile (Work sample reference)
    5. Brief proposed methodology, work plan, and timeline
    6. Financial quotation (Lump sum, inclusive of all applicable costs)
    7. Contact details     
  • Timeline and Diuration:

Will be discussed during the contract signing.Expression of Interest Dubbing

Deadline for Submission: June 22, 2026.

Electronic submissions should be sent in PDF format via email with the subject line "EOI: DBI Website Design Services - [Your Entity Name]" to: ( hr@digobikas.org )

For technical or administrative inquiries regarding this call, contact: Digo Bikas Institute (DBI)

Puja Prathisthan Margh, Mid Baneshwor, Kathmandu, Nepal

Phone: +977-01-4610656

DBI reserves the right to accept or reject any or all expressions of interest without assigning any reason whatsoever. Only shortlisted candidates/firms will be contacted for the secondary Request for Proposal (RFP) process, which will include detailed financial bidding
